data.title: The case for the F-Series being the most dominant vehicle line in auto history
data.description:
Ford's (F) F-Series pickup truck is firmly established as one of the most durable franchises in U.S. automotive history. Notably, the F-Series has maintained its position as the country’s best-selling pickup since 1977 and the best-selling vehicle overall since 1981. The unprecedented streak for the auto industry reflects a combination of product breadth, brand equity, and deep integration into both retail and commercial customer bases. 

What began in 1948 as Ford's (F) first modern, dedicated truck line has evolved into a high-volume, high-margin cornerstone of the company’s North American business strategy and a key component of the Detroit automaker's earnings power.

From its early generations, the F-Series differentiated itself with a truck-specific chassis and configurations designed for genuine work use, rather than simply adapting passenger-car platforms. Over time, Ford expanded the lineup from light-duty models to include Super Duty variants, capturing heavy-duty, towing-intensive, and fleet applications across construction, agriculture, logistics, and government. 

The large installed F-Series base supports robust resale values, extensive parts availability, and a broad servicing infrastructure, all of which reinforce customer loyalty and lower switching incentives. As the franchise matured, Ford layered in higher-trim and luxury offerings, transforming the F-Series from a pure work tool into a high-content, high-transaction-price product that appeals to both small businesses and affluent retail buyers.

U.S. F-Series volume has remained in the 700,000–800,000 unit range annually, with recent years approaching or exceeding the upper end of that band despite cyclical headwinds, supply disruptions, and intensifying competition in the full-size pickup segment. The line’s mix increasingly includes higher-margin models and technology packages, while the introduction of the F-150 Lightning added an EV option.

 

 

Looking forward, the continued dominance of the F-Series in the U.S. market appears likely, although GM’s Chevrolet Silverado, GMC Sierra, and Stellantis’s Ram have been formidable challengers. 

Cumulatively, Ford (F) has sold more than 30M F-Series trucks globally, putting it among the three all-time best-selling automotive nameplates alongside the Toyota Corolla (TM) and Volkswagen Golf (VLKAF).

As for profits, the F-Series, particularly the high‑trim F‑150/Super Duty trucks, delivers lush margins compared with Ford's (F) cars and the electric vehicle program.

data.title: U.S. government enters partial shutdown, but deal seen soon
data.description:
The federal government entered a partial shutdown early Saturday morning, even after the Senate passed a funding package hours earlier. The shutdown is expected to be brief, as the House of Representatives is set to vote on the measure when it reconvenes Monday.

Senate Passes Funding Package

The Senate approved a legislative package by a 71-29 vote that includes five appropriations bills along with a two-week stopgap measure. However, the House must also approve the deal before it can be sent to President Donald Trump for his signature.

Since the House isn't scheduled to return to Washington until Monday, the government entered what officials hope will be a short-lived shutdown. This follows last year's record 43-day shutdown.

What's Affected

Appropriations expired Saturday for multiple government departments and agencies, including:

  • State Department
  • Defense Department
  • Department of Homeland Security
  • Labor Department
  • Health and Human Services
  • Transportation Department
  • Education Department
  • Housing and Urban Development
  • Financial Services and related programs

Russell Vought, Director of the Office of Management and Budget, instructed federal agency heads to have employees report for their next scheduled shift to conduct "orderly shutdown activities." He expressed hope that the lapse would be brief.

DHS Funding Stripped Out

A key element of the Senate agreement was the removal of long-term funding for the Department of Homeland Security from the main package. Instead, DHS will be temporarily funded through a stopgap measure, with its long-term appropriations to be addressed later.

This decision comes amid intense Democratic criticism of DHS over aggressive immigration enforcement actions in Minnesota.

Republican Holdout Delays Process

The Senate vote was delayed by Republican Sen. Lindsey Graham of South Carolina, who placed a hold on the measure. Graham demanded a guaranteed vote on his bill to criminalize sanctuary city policies, which would impose criminal penalties on state and local officials who interfere with federal immigration enforcement.

Graham also sought an amendment related to the Arctic Frost investigation by former special counsel Jack Smith, requiring officials to notify senators if their phone records are obtained during criminal investigations.

Path Forward

House Speaker Mike Johnson indicated on a GOP conference call Friday that he would support the Senate-passed deal, citing President Trump's backing of the agreement. Johnson said he hopes the House will pass the bill Monday.

Trump encouraged lawmakers to support the deal in a Truth Social post Thursday. The funding package would keep most of the federal government operational through the end of the fiscal year on September 30.

Once the House approves the measure, it will be sent to President Trump for his signature, ending the shutdown.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Worst performing consumer staples stocks in the past month
data.description:
As the first month of 2026 comes to an end this week and earnings season accelerates, below is a list of the worst performing consumer staples stocks with market capitalizations of $10 billion or more in the past month. The list is ranked based on price performance.

The list is topped by McCormick & Company (MKC), with a one month performance of -11.31%. Dollar Tree (DLTR) and General Mills (GIS) are next, with Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) and The Kraft Heinz Company (KHC) rounding out the rest of the top five.

The stocks on this list carry a range of Quant Ratings, from McCormick & Company (MKC) with a Sell rating of 1.82 to Dollar Tree (DLTR) with a Strong Buy rating of 4.61. Other notable inclusions are Keurig Dr Pepper Inc. (KDP) and The Kraft Heinz Company (KHC), both carrying Hold ratings of 2.87 and 2.96, respectively.

Here is the list:

  1. McCormick & Company (MKC), 1 month performance percentage: -11.31%

  2. Dollar Tree (DLTR), 1 month performance percentage: -5.34%

  3. General Mills (GIS), 1 month performance percentage: -5.31%

  4. Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP), 1 month performance percentage: -4.76%

  5. The Kraft Heinz Company (KHC), 1 month performance percentage: -4.58%

  6. Kimberly-Clark (KMB), 1 month performance percentage: -2.35%

  7. The Kroger Co. (KR), 1 month performance percentage: -1.53%

  8. BJ’s Wholesale Club Holdings (BJ), 1 month performance percentage: -1.31%

  9. Kenvue (KVUE), 1 month performance percentage: 0.06%

  10. Hormel Foods (HRL), 1 month performance percentage: 1.12%

Consumer Staples ETFs: (XLP), (VDC), (IYK), (FSTA), (KXI), and (RSPS)

data.title: Cavco signals $10M synergy target from American Homestar integration as retail margins compress
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cavco Industries (CVCO) Q3 2026

Management View

  • CEO William Boor began by highlighting the impact of the American Homestar acquisition on the quarter, noting, "The year-over-year EPS decrease is best dissected starting from the bottom of the income statement. Our tax rate was considerably higher than a year ago, partly due to declining tax credits from the phasing out of the Energy Star program and partly due to nondeductible deal costs." Boor emphasized that the integration of American Homestar is proceeding according to plan, with "impressive execution from HR benefits and payroll to finance, IT and operations." The company now sees "tangible and measurable synergies... above $10 million on an annual basis, and we estimate that about half has been achieved in the run rate as we entered Q4." Boor also reported continued share repurchases, with $44 million used to buy back company stock during the quarter.
  • CFO Allison Aden stated, "Net revenue for the third fiscal quarter of 2026 was $581 million, up $59 million or 11.3% from $522 million in the prior year quarter." Aden also noted, "Consolidated gross margin as a percentage of net revenue was 23.4%, down from 24.9% in the same period last year." She attributed the decrease in gross margin to "higher per unit cost" and highlighted that "the American Homestar operating costs are expected to decline as we realize projected synergies."
  • Chief Accounting Officer Paul Bigbee reported, "In the quarter, we had a decrease in cash and restricted cash of $157.5 million, bringing our balance to $242.5 million. Cash provided by operating activities was $66.1 million."

Outlook

  • Management indicated no formal forward guidance, but Boor stated, "The bias in our plants generally is to hold pace and go up from here whenever orders and backlogs allow." He also noted that backlogs were "in the 4 to 6 weeks range" and that "early indications are that backlogs are stable and could increase or if we pick up production pace, be maintained at this level heading into the spring."
  • CFO Aden shared, "As the synergies begin to take hold and we see an uptick there, we'll also see the integration costs continue to decrease slightly as we go forward."

Financial Results

  • CFO Aden reported, "Net revenue for the third fiscal quarter of 2026 was $581 million, up $59 million or 11.3% from $522 million in the prior year quarter. Sequentially, net revenues increased $24.5 million, driven by the addition of American Homestar, which contributed $42 million... partially offset by a reduction in base business units sold."
  • The Factory-Built Housing segment recorded net revenue of $558.5 million, up $57.6 million from the prior year quarter, while Financial Services segment net revenue was $22.5 million, up $1.3 million.
  • Gross margin as a percentage of net revenue was 23.4%, down from 24.9% in the prior year. Selling, general and administrative expenses were $81.4 million, up from $66 million, reflecting $6.9 million in American Homestar operating costs and $2.9 million in deal-related expenses.
  • Net income was $44.1 million, and diluted earnings per share was $5.58, compared to $6.90 in the same quarter last year.

Q&A

  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: Asked about production utilization and expectations for Q4. Boor responded, "Our plants right now because we maintain that production rate are just in a really nice spot to be able to continue moving up if we get the spring selling season we're hoping and expecting."
  • Moore also inquired about gross margin drivers. Aden explained, "There was no... impact to gross margins from the acquisition. And when we think about margins year-over-year, they were down due to increases in input costs."
  • Greg Palm, Craig-Hallum: Queried about relative weakness in the communities channel. Boor stated, "When we look at the volume decrease we saw, it was pretty focused on the community side... I'm still not hearing communities with pessimism or feeling that if they set another house, they won't be able to find either a buyer or a renter for that house."
  • Jesse Lederman, Zelman & Associates: Asked about backlog stability and spring outlook. Boor replied, "We have not slowed... we didn't slow in the third quarter as far as production rate. And we haven't slowed... here early January."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to cautiously optimistic tone, probing for clarity on gross margin, channel performance, and synergy realization, but not expressing overt skepticism.
  • Management’s sentiment was optimistic during both prepared remarks and Q&A, with Boor frequently referencing operational readiness for a stronger spring and describing integration progress as "impressive." Management acknowledged short-term margin pressures and integration costs, but emphasized long-term positioning and synergy realization.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management tone remained largely stable, with a slightly more positive outlook on integration benefits and market positioning.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter was marked by the closing and integration of American Homestar, with management now quantifying expected annual synergies at above $10 million, a more concrete target than previously discussed.
  • Gross margin declined from 24.2% in Q2 to 23.4% in Q3, attributed to higher per-unit costs and retail price compression, while in Q2, margins had benefited from low lumber costs.
  • The previous quarter saw regional shipment strength in the North and concerns about Southeastern softness; Q3 saw Southeast stability and relative weakness in communities as a channel.
  • Average selling price continued to rise, supported by a shift toward multi-section homes and greater integration of retail operations.
  • Management confidence in operational flexibility and market positioning remained high in both quarters, with increasing focus on integration synergies and capital allocation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management called out retail margin compression, noting it was "localized" and not representative of broader trends.
  • Higher per unit costs and tariff impacts were cited as ongoing risks to gross margin.
  • Analysts raised questions about the sustainability of recent gross margin trends, production utilization, and the pace of synergy realization from the acquisition.
  • Management described integration costs as having peaked in Q3 and anticipated a decline in subsequent quarters, with synergies expected to offset these costs moving forward.

Final Takeaway

Cavco Industries highlighted successful initial integration of American Homestar, setting an annual synergy target above $10 million and reporting that approximately half of this run rate has been achieved as of Q4 entry. Despite year-over-year pressures on gross margin and earnings per share, management emphasized operational readiness for a potential spring market rebound, stable backlogs, and continued strategic investments, including share repurchases. Retail margin compression and input cost pressures remain key risks, but management expressed confidence that synergy realization and market positioning will support future performance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crude oil posts biggest weekly gains since October as market watches Iran
data.description:
Crude oil futures ended little changed Friday but wrapped up their largest weekly gains since October, as the buildup of U.S. military forces in the Middle East heightened concerns that strikes against Iran could occur soon, potentially disrupting global crude supply.

An escalation between the U.S. and Iran would place ~1.5M bbl/day of Iranian oil exports at risk and spark worries over broader Persian Gulf oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

"It's really all about Iran right now" regarding oil prices, Again Capital's John Kilduff said in a note. "The market had priced in a lot of geopolitical risk on Iran, but it's difficult to quantify the market at this point. The question is if there is action against Iran, what will the Iranians do?"

Oil stabilized Friday after President Trump told reporters that Iran wants to make a deal, possibly a signal that he may now be leaning toward diplomacy rather than military action in the region.

Prices also have been lifted by supply disruptions elsewhere, including a significant loss of crude exports from Kazakhstan due to damage at the Caspian Pipeline Consortium, although flows are expected to recover in the coming months.

The massive winter storm that slammed much of the U.S. this week has supported demand for heating fuels and disrupted production on the Gulf Coast and elsewhere, but the overall fundamentals outlook remains bearish due to growing oversupply.

"The oil market remains well supplied, although the oversupply is not quite as high as initially assumed thanks to supply disruptions and slightly stronger demand," Commerzbank's Barbara Lambrecht said in a note. "Inventory data next week should provide insight into the extent of the disruptions in the U.S."

Key OPEC+ members are set to meet virtually on Sunday, with no changes to production policy expected for now.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for March delivery closed -0.3% to $65.21/bbl, snapping a three-session winning streak, and front-month March Brent crude (CO1:COM) fell $0.02 to $70.69/bbl; Nymex and Brent rallied 6.8% and 7.3%, respectively, for the week, and jumped 13.6% and 16.2%, respectively, in January.

U.S. natural gas (NG1:COM) rose for the eighth time in nine sessions as continuing frigid weather keeps near-term demand expectations high while production has not fully recovered from major freeze-offs earlier in the week; front-month Nymex gas for March delivery settled Friday +11.1% at $4.354/MMBtu, while surging 20.6% for the week and 18.1% for the month.

Energy stocks, as represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE), finished the week +3.8%.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Exxon, Chevron see hope for Venezuela but sweeping changes needed
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX) offered insights into their perspectives about Venezuela during earnings conference calls on Friday, even as neither company announced long-term investment commitments despite ​President Trump's push to convince U.S. oil companies to pump $100B into the country's energy sector.

Exxon (XOM) CEO ‌Darren Woods touted his company's technological capability to potentially extract Venezuela's expensive heavy crude for a lower cost, while Chevron (CVX) CEO Mike Wirth said his company would process more Venezuelan crude through its refineries in the U.S., and both bosses said they wanted to see strong legal frameworks and a stable political environment before making decisions about long-term projects.

Woods, who called Venezuela "uninvestable" at a White House meeting earlier this month, said he believes the Trump administration is working to stabilize the country, boost its economy and transition into a democratic government, and said Exxon (XOM) offers technological capabilities to pump Venezuela's heavy crude, because it produces similar crude in Canada.

"We think we bring an advantaged approach that will lead to lower-cost production [and] higher recovery, Woods said on Exxon's (XOM) earnings call. "That's the opportunity set for us that will play out, maybe over time," adding that the company still plans to send a technical team to assess the situation in Venezuela.

Chevron (CVX) could increase Venezuelan heavy crude flows in its U.S. refining system, but that it is too early to determine the company's long-term outlook on the country, CEO Mike Wirth said on his company's earnings call, noting the company currently processes ~50K bbl/day at its Pascagoula refinery in Mississippi and could run an additional 100K bbl/day across its Gulf Coast refineries and on the West Coast at El Segundo in California.

"There will be a number of signposts that we'll be watching," Wirth said. "It will have to compete in our portfolio versus attractive investments in many other parts of the world. With the right changes, we certainly could see our operations in the footprint expand in Venezuela."

Exxon (XOM) and Chevron (CVX) both reported their smallest annual profits since 2021, pressured by a growing glut of crude that has weighed on prices.

data.title: BE Resources announces private placement of 2.6M common shares
data.description:
• BE Resources (BER.H:CA) announced on Friday that it is proposing to complete a non-brokered private placement of 2,600,000 common shares at $0.05 each to raise aggregate gross proceeds up to $130,000.
  • The offering remains subject to various closing conditions, including the approval of the TSX Venture Exchange, the company said. 
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Cannabist Company enters forbearance agreement with senior noteholders
data.description:
• The Cannabist Company Holdings (Cboe CA: CBST) (OTCQB: CBSTF) said on Friday it has entered into a forbearance agreement with noteholders representing more than 75% of its senior secured notes due in 2028.
  • The agreement follows the company’s decision not to make interest payments due on December 31, 2025, which became an event of default after the expiration of a 30-day grace period.
  • Under the forbearance agreement, the noteholders agreed to refrain from exercising remedies related to the missed payments until February 17, 2026.
  • The company said it is continuing discussions with noteholders regarding options to address its liquidity needs, including potential asset sales or other strategic or financial transactions.
  • CBSTF closed -8.31% at $0.043.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: First Business projects 10% annual growth in loans, deposits, and revenue for 2026 while boosting dividend 17%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Business Financial Services, Inc. (FBIZ) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Corey Chambas, CEO, stated that "First Business Bank finished 2025 with another outstanding quarter. Our team continued to produce high-quality growth, particularly on the deposit side. Core net interest margin remained resilient and our revenue streams were diversified and strong." Chambas highlighted that "Private Wealth business continued to expand, delivering record and significant annuity-like fee income," and that "pretax pre-provision earnings grew nearly 15% over 2024, return on average tangible common equity was over 15% for the year." He also reported that "tangible book value per share grew 14% from a year ago" and that "2025 EPS grew 14% over 2024, exceeding our long-term annual goal of 10% earnings growth."

  • Chambas announced a "17% increase to our quarterly cash dividend" and emphasized the company’s track record of double-digit growth, aiming to continue this in 2026.

  • David Seiler, President & COO, addressed an isolated credit situation: "During the quarter, we downgraded $20.4 million of CRE loans related to a single Wisconsin-based borrower with total loans outstanding of $29.7 million." He explained that this was due to the borrower's inability to advance parcels for multifamily development, but noted, "land value appraisals exceed the carrying value of the loans. As such, a specific reserve was not recorded."

  • Seiler pointed out that "Private Wealth generated a record $3.8 million of fee income, up 11% year-over-year" and that "service charges were up nearly 20% year-over-year." He also noted that "core deposit balances were up 12% from both the linked and prior year quarters."

  • Brian Spielmann, CFO, stated, "Fourth quarter net interest margin declined by 15 basis points to 3.53%, reflecting 10 basis points of compression from the nonaccrual interest reversal on the downgraded CRE nonperforming loan. Excluding this, net interest margin would have measured 3.63%." He added, "On a full year basis, net interest margin remained relatively stable, declining 2 basis points from 3.66% in 2024 to 3.64% in 2025."

  • Spielmann explained an accounting change: "In the fourth quarter, we reclassified $904,000 out of noninterest expense and into other noninterest income to net against the related revenue. Excluding this reclassification, income from partnership investments decreased $383,000 to $477,000 during the fourth quarter."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated a target of 10% annual growth in net interest income, loans, deposits, and revenue for 2026. Spielmann said, "Our target range for net interest margin is unchanged. Our current outlook supports this in tandem with double-digit annual loan, deposit and revenue growth."

  • Seiler forecasted, "We would expect to see growth rebound to our typical double-digit pace in 2026," noting strong loan demand and expanding pipelines.

  • Spielmann added, "We expect 2026 compensation levels to grow a bit more than in 2025" and confirmed, "Our 2025 effective tax rate of 16.8% was within our expected annual range of 16% to 18%, and we continue to believe this range is appropriate looking forward."

Financial Results

  • Chambas highlighted that "EPS growth is perhaps the most universal metric across industries, and our track record is outstanding."

  • Seiler noted, "Loan balances grew about $39 million or 5% annualized during the quarter and $261 million or 8% over the same period last year."

  • Seiler emphasized, "Private Wealth generated a record $3.8 million of fee income, up 11% year-over-year," and "service charges were up nearly 20% year-over-year."

  • Spielmann reported, "Fourth quarter net interest margin declined by 15 basis points to 3.53%."

  • Seiler stated, "Net charge-offs totaled $2.5 million and were primarily from previously reserved equipment finance loans."

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked about CRE appraisals and loan-to-value. Chambas responded, "Most of the appraisals, we just got several in just now at the end of the year... overall loan-to-value across those seven properties is 72% on the LTV."

  • Tamayo queried about credit costs. Chambas clarified, "The charge-offs that we had for the quarter were not related to this. So based on those appraisals, we didn't have to take even any reserves on this."

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about the timeline for resolution of the problem loan. Chambas said, "We do think because there are multiple pieces of real estate here that there can be shorter-term progress potentially with some pieces of this, even in the very near term."

  • Rulis asked about equipment finance balances. Chambas answered, "We were at $21 million at the end of the quarter. And I think that went down about $20 million over the course of the year."

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Inquired about margin starting point and loan mix. Spielmann replied, "When you adjust for the nonaccrual interest in Q4, that resets us at 3.63%. And with that mix that we're seeing in the pipelines, we feel like it's a great place to be and within our range of 3.60% to 3.65%."

  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Asked about expense growth. Spielmann answered, "Compensation is going to increase a little bit more than 7.5% this year, there's not much left for the rest of the expenses, and that's consistent with our approach to generating annual positive operating leverage."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on credit quality, problem loan resolution timing, and fee income guidance, with a slightly cautious but constructive tone.

  • Management maintained a confident and positive tone, emphasizing strong execution and the ability to sustain growth, though acknowledging isolated credit issues and competitive deposit pricing. Chambas stated, "We know how to execute to achieve our double-digit growth mandate, and we aim to continue doing so in 2026 and beyond."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, the management tone remains confident and consistent, while analysts' questions showed a slightly increased focus on credit risk and loan growth sustainability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance remains consistent, reiterating double-digit growth targets in loans, deposits, and revenue.

  • The isolated credit event in CRE this quarter contrasts with the previous quarter's stable asset quality.

  • Loan growth moderated in Q4 compared to stronger Q3, due to higher CRE payoffs, but management expects rebound in 2026.

  • Fee income drivers shifted from unusually high swap and SBIC income last quarter to record Private Wealth and service charges this quarter.

  • Management's tone and confidence in sustaining growth is unchanged, though analyst focus has shifted more toward credit quality and payoff trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management addressed a $20.4 million CRE loan downgrade related to a single borrower, emphasizing that the issue is isolated and collateral value exceeds carrying value.

  • Seiler reported, "Outside of the new and isolated nonaccrual relationship, the balance of our portfolio continues to perform as expected, and we have no areas of particular concern."

  • Spielmann noted, "Deposit pricing pressure has eased modestly since the Fed began cutting, the cost of acquiring a new deposit client remains extremely competitive."

  • Analysts expressed concerns about the duration and impact of credit resolution and future payoff activity in CRE.

Final Takeaway

First Business Financial Services closed 2025 with robust profitability, highlighted by double-digit annual growth in key metrics, a 17% dividend increase, and continued execution on its long-term growth mandate. Management remains confident in sustaining 10% annual growth in loans, deposits, and revenue for 2026, despite isolated credit headwinds, and sees positive trends in core deposit and fee income expansion supporting ongoing shareholder value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
